expectations.text: Explains FedRAMP 20x as a modernization/automation-focused initiative
expectations.evidence: Response clearly explains FedRAMP 20x as the primary authorization pathway focused on modernization, shifting from document-heavy point-in-time assessments to continuous, automated, machine-readable approaches.

expectations.text: Mentions key changes: automation, machine-readable controls (OSCAL), continuous monitoring improvements
expectations.evidence: Response has dedicated subsections for: continuous authorization, machine-readable controls via OSCAL, modular API-driven submissions, automated evidence collection, and ConMon integration — all key changes are covered.

expectations.text: Addresses the current status and timeline of 20x rollout
expectations.evidence: Response includes a timeline table with specific milestones: FedRAMP Ready retirement (July 28, 2026), OSCAL mandate (September 30, 2026), JAB P-ATO suspension, and notes CR26 class baselines are being published.

expectations.text: Provides practical guidance on path selection
expectations.evidence: Response has a dedicated section 'Should You Pursue FedRAMP 20x or Traditional FedRAMP?' with clear criteria for each path and a bottom-line recommendation favoring FedRAMP 20x for new authorizations.

expectations.text: Mentions OSCAL (Open Security Controls Assessment Language)
expectations.evidence: OSCAL is mentioned extensively: as a key change, with September 30, 2026 mandate deadline, explanation of what OSCAL enables (automated validation, machine-to-machine exchange, reuse), and practical first steps for adopting OSCAL tooling.
